compiled_state.title: [/dso:sprint review dispatch]: orchestrator invokes code review with subagent_type=dso:code-reviewer-* -> generic general-purpose agent runs instead of named specialist
compiled_state.author: Test
compiled_state.description:
## Observed Behavior (host project, sprint 91f5-0aec Batch 2, 2026-04-16)

During /dso:sprint Phase 5 Step 7 (Formal Code Review), the orchestrator dispatched a generic `general-purpose` sub-agent for the light-tier review even though specialized named agents (`dso:code-reviewer-light`, `dso:code-reviewer-standard`, `dso:code-reviewer-deep-*`, etc.) are defined in `plugins/dso/agents/` and referenced throughout CLAUDE.md (agent dispatch table).

The Agent tool ran with `subagent_type: dso:code-reviewer-light` and completed successfully — but the resulting review invocation was observed by the user as "generic agent", suggesting the `subagent_type` parameter may have been silently ignored, degraded to `general-purpose`, or the dispatched agent lacked the specialized prompt/persona the named agent is supposed to carry.

## Hypothesis
Possible causes worth investigating:
1. **Name mismatch** — the agent metadata file (`plugins/dso/agents/code-reviewer-light.md`) may define a different `name:` frontmatter value than what the orchestrator and REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md invoke as `subagent_type`.
2. **Registration gap** — the harness/plugin registry may not be picking up the `dso:code-reviewer-*` agent definitions at session start, silently falling back to `general-purpose`.
3. **Cache drift** — the plugin cache under `~/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/digital-service-orchestra/` may be serving an older agent list than the source worktree.
4. **Permission flow** — the Agent tool may accept the `subagent_type` parameter without validating it against the registered agent list, logging success even when no matching agent exists.

## Reproduction
1. Start `/dso:sprint <epic-id>` on an epic with a ready batch.
2. Complete a batch.
3. In Phase 5 Step 7, observe the code review sub-agent dispatch. Look for visible signals that the specialized agent's prompt/persona is in effect (e.g., adherence to REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md dimension scoring schema, use of `reviewer-findings.json` contract).
4. Compare against the named-agent dispatch table in CLAUDE.md — the review agent should match one of `dso:code-reviewer-light|standard|deep-correctness|deep-verification|deep-hygiene|deep-arch` and (when overlays trigger) `dso:code-reviewer-security-red-team|blue-team`, `dso:code-reviewer-performance`, `dso:code-reviewer-test-quality`.

## Expected Behavior
The orchestrator should dispatch a named agent from the `dso:code-reviewer-*` family (selected by `review-complexity-classifier.sh` output) and the Agent tool should route to the matching specialized agent definition. If no agent matches, the tool should emit a clear error rather than silently falling back to `general-purpose`.

## Investigation Starting Points
- `plugins/dso/agents/` — list files and their `name:` frontmatter.
- `.claude/` / plugin cache — confirm the same agent names are registered.
- `plugins/dso/docs/workflows/REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md` — check that `subagent_type` strings in Step 4 match agent definitions exactly.
- `plugins/dso/scripts/review-complexity-classifier.sh` — verify it emits exact agent names.
- Agent tool behavior when `subagent_type` is unknown: does it error or fall back?

## Impact
If reviews are silently routed to `general-purpose`, tier selection (haiku/sonnet/opus) and dimension-specific review behavior are lost, degrading review quality without a visible failure signal.

## Related
- CLAUDE.md "Named-agent dispatch" table
- CLAUDE.md rule 12: "Never manually call record-review.sh" — depends on the named agent writing reviewer-findings.json

comments.body: Triaged (debug-everything Bug-Fix Mode): bug targets an LLM-behavioral skill/agent/prompt file. Per CLAUDE.md rule 22 and behavioral-testing-standard Rule 5, any modification requires dedicated bot-psychologist investigation and explicit user approval. comments.body: PARTIAL_FIX (commit 616a684a): CLAUDE.md agent dispatch table now clarifies dso:* labels are file identifiers, not subagent_type values. Sprint SKILL.md Phase 5 Step 7 explicit callout still pending. Keeping open pending sprint SKILL.md edit.
comments.author: Test

comments.body: Investigation (cluster C, sonnet, KERNEL): Taxonomy #10 Brittle API Mapping + #16 Instruction Locality. ROOT_CAUSE: CLAUDE.md named-agent dispatch table lists `dso:code-reviewer-*` as if they were valid `subagent_type` values — but Agent tool only accepts built-in types (general-purpose, Explore, Plan). Orchestrators mapping `dso:code-reviewer-*` → `subagent_type: 'dso:code-reviewer-*'` cause Agent tool to silently fall back to general-purpose, producing a generic agent that lacks the named reviewer's prompt. REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md Step 4's correction is 200+ lines deep — not reached when CLAUDE.md table appears authoritative. PARTIAL FIX APPLIED (commit 616a684a): CLAUDE.md dispatch table header now clarifies `dso:*` labels are file identifiers, not subagent_type. REMAINING: sprint SKILL.md Phase 5 Step 7 should add inline callout "DISPATCH PATTERN: Read REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md Step 4 inline before dispatching. Use subagent_type: 'general-purpose' — NOT the dso:code-reviewer-* label." Confidence: high. Minimal-fix applied: true.
comments.author: Test

comments.body: USER_APPROVAL (2026-04-20): User directive — 'Fixes recommended by bot-psychologist are approved' for all Category D intent-confirmed clusters. Remaining fix approved: sprint SKILL.md Phase 5 Step 7 inline callout "DISPATCH PATTERN: Read REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md Step 4 inline before dispatching. Use subagent_type: 'general-purpose' — NOT the dso:code-reviewer-* label."
comments.author: Test
